behaviors.description: The content passed to the summary-generation step includes data retrieved from all three sources.
behaviors.justification: The orchestration layer must correctly collect from all three sources and aggregate their results. While a unit test with mocks could verify function calls, it would miss real bugs in data aggregation that arise from handling actual API responses (malformed data, missing fields, incompatible structures). An integration test with realistic test doubles for the three sources catches aggregation failures without requiring real external APIs.
behaviors.test_description: Set up test doubles (mock HTTP servers or stubbed responses) for the metrics endpoint, issue tracker API, and deployment log that return representative data structures. Call the real orchestration/collection code. Verify that the data structure passed to the summary generator contains all three sources' data with expected keys and values. Test variations including empty responses, large datasets, and missing optional fields to ensure the aggregation is robust.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration-level testing with test doubles allows verification of real aggregation logic without external service dependencies. A unit test with pure mocks might pass even if one source is silently dropped during aggregation, so Integration is the minimum level to catch these bugs.

behaviors.description: When one data source is unavailable, the report is produced with a 'data unavailable' note for that section rather than aborting.
behaviors.justification: The script must handle graceful degradation when a source fails (timeout, network error, HTTP error). A unit test mocking an exception only tests the error handler in isolation, missing real bugs in how the orchestration handles partial failures across multiple sources simultaneously. An integration test with a test double that realistically simulates unavailability (raising timeout, returning error response) exercises the full error-handling and aggregation path.
behaviors.test_description: Set up test doubles for the three sources, with one configured to raise a timeout exception or return an HTTP 5xx error. Call the orchestration code. Verify that: (1) the script completes without aborting, (2) remaining sources' data is included in the report, (3) a 'data unavailable' note appears for the failed source. Repeat this test with each source failing individually and with multiple sources failing simultaneously to ensure robustness.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration-level testing with realistic failure simulation ensures error handling is correct across the orchestration layer. Unit tests with mocks might pass while missing real failures in how the system handles concurrent or cascading source failures.

behaviors.description: The narrative summary accurately represents the key values present in the input data.
behaviors.justification: If the summary is generated by an LLM, accuracy depends on the real model's non-deterministic behavior, which can omit key values, hallucinate details, or misrepresent data. Mocking the LLM to return a fixed 'good' summary completely bypasses accuracy testing and hides real failures. A unit or integration test with a mock LLM would always pass, hiding hallucinations, omissions, and misinterpretations. Only running the real LLM with test inputs and automated/manual evaluation can catch these real failures.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real LLM API with structured test data containing specific metrics, numbers, and domain details. Generate summaries. Evaluate the output using automated checks (e.g., presence of all key metrics, verification that numerical values match input data, checking for obvious hallucinations) and/or human review. Run the test multiple times with the same input to catch non-deterministic failures. Include edge cases: missing optional fields, very large numbers, conflicting or ambiguous data.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Agentic-level testing with the real LLM is necessary because LLM accuracy is inherently non-deterministic and cannot be verified through mocks. Mocking would hide hallucinations, data omissions, and subtle misrepresentations that occur in real LLM behavior.

behaviors.description: A complete report — from data collection through email delivery — reaches the configured recipient's inbox correctly.
behaviors.justification: This end-to-end behavior requires testing real email delivery (SMTP server, authentication, email formatting, recipient parsing). While intermediate steps (data collection, summary generation) might use test doubles, the final delivery step requires real SMTP infrastructure. A unit or integration test with mocked email delivery would hide real failures like SMTP authentication issues, email format rejection by mail servers, TLS/encryption problems, or recipient address parsing bugs. System-level testing with a real or test SMTP server is the minimum to catch these delivery failures.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a test SMTP server (e.g., MailHog, local Postfix, or AWS SES test mode). Configure the script to deliver to the test server. Run the complete pipeline: collect data (using real or test doubles), generate summary (with real LLM or a production-like mock), format email with full headers, and send via SMTP. Verify that: (1) email is received by the test SMTP server, (2) email content and structure are correct, (3) recipient address is correctly formatted, (4) email headers are valid, (5) the message can be parsed by a mail client library.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: System-level testing with a test SMTP server is necessary to verify real email delivery behavior. Integration tests with a mocked SMTP client would miss format issues, authentication failures, and protocol-level problems.

behaviors.description: When the email delivery service is unreachable, the generated report is saved to a local file and delivery is retried on the next scheduled run.
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires testing both real email service unavailability and the retry mechanism across scheduler boundaries. A unit test with a mocked SMTP client never tests unavailability scenarios. An integration test can verify file persistence logic but would miss real failures in the scheduler's retry orchestration, state persistence between runs, or timing issues. A system-level test with an actually unreachable SMTP server is needed to verify the complete pipeline: detect unavailability, save report to file, and ensure the scheduled retry actually triggers, reads the saved state, and succeeds on the next run.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a test SMTP server that is initially offline/unresponsive. Run the script's data collection and email delivery. Verify that: (1) SMTP connection fails, (2) the report is saved to a configured file path with recoverable state (metadata, original input), (3) the script logs retry intent. Bring the SMTP server online. Manually trigger the next scheduled run. Verify that: (4) the retry mechanism reads the saved report, (5) sends it via email successfully, (6) deletes or archives the temporary file, (7) logs success.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: System-level testing is necessary to verify both the actual file-save error path and the scheduler's retry mechanism working together. Integration tests would miss scheduler-level retry failures, and unit tests would not test real SMTP unavailability scenarios.
